data.content:
Here are the two incremental build issues found in `IncrementalBroken.csproj`:

---

### 1. `GenerateTimestamp` (line 6)
- **Missing `Inputs`/`Outputs`** — runs unconditionally every build.
- **Volatile property** — `$([System.DateTime]::Now.ToString())` changes every invocation, so even with `Inputs`/`Outputs` it would always produce a different file, invalidating downstream targets like `CoreCompile`.
- **Not registered in `FileWrites`** — `build-timestamp.txt` won't be cleaned by `dotnet clean`.

### 2. `EmbedGitHash` (line 13)
- **Missing `Inputs`/`Outputs`** — runs unconditionally every build.
- **Overwrites file every time** — even when `git rev-parse HEAD` returns the same hash, `Overwrite="true"` updates the file timestamp on `git-hash.g.cs`, which makes `CoreCompile` think its inputs changed and triggers a full recompile.
- **Not registered in `FileWrites`** — generated `git-hash.g.cs` won't be cleaned.

---

### How to fix

**`GenerateTimestamp`**: Remove or make it only run in CI. A volatile timestamp defeats incremental builds by design.

**`EmbedGitHash`**: Add `Inputs`/`Outputs` and only write the file when the content actually changes:

```xml
<Target Name="EmbedGitHash" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile"
        Inputs="$(ProjectPath)" Outputs="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs">
  <Exec Command="git rev-parse HEAD" ConsoleToMsBuild="true" IgnoreExitCode="true">
    <Output TaskParameter="ConsoleOutput" PropertyName="GitHash" />
  </Exec>
  <!-- Write to a temp file, then compare to avoid unnecessary timestamp changes -->
  <WriteLinesToFile File="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs.tmp" Lines="..." Overwrite="true" />
  <Copy SourceFiles="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs.tmp"
        DestinationFiles="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs"
        SkipUnchangedFiles="true" />
  <ItemGroup>
    <FileWrites Include="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Target>
```

The core pattern: **every custom target needs `Inputs`/`Outputs`**, should avoid volatile content, and must use `SkipUnchangedFiles` or content-comparison to prevent unnecessary downstream rebuilds.
data.reasoningText:
Let me analyze the two custom targets for incremental build issues.

**Target 1: `GenerateTimestamp`**
- No `Inputs`/`Outputs` attributes → always runs
- Writes a timestamp file with `$([System.DateTime]::Now.ToString())` — this is a **volatile property** that changes every build
- Even if you added `Inputs`/`Outputs`, the content changes every time, so it would always be considered dirty
- This writes to `build-timestamp.txt` which isn't registered in `FileWrites` for clean support

**Target 2: `EmbedGitHash`**


- Also lacks `Inputs`/`Outputs`, so it always executes
- Runs `git rev-parse HEAD` and regenerates the C# file each time, updating its timestamp even when the hash hasn't changed
- This causes downstream targets like `CoreCompile` to think their inputs have changed, breaking incremental builds
- The generated file isn't tracked in `FileWrites` either

The core issue is that both targets always re-execute and produce files that either have volatile content or get unnecessarily rewritten, defeating incremental build caching.
